link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 水口神社 |画像 = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地= {{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|甲賀市|Q847584}}{{jalink|水口町|Q3314706}}宮の前3-14 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=水口神社 (甲賀市)}} |祭神 = 大水口宿禰命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 伝・{{jalink|垂仁天皇|Q314850}}朝 |本殿 = 一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |例祭 = {{jalink|4月20日|Q2524}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=水口神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''水口神社'''（みなくちじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|甲賀市|Q847584}}{{jalink|水口町|Q3314706}}宮の前にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}。旧社格は{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|県社}}。例祭として{{jalink|水口曳山祭|Q11548378}}を行う。 === 社名 === 中世には「山王新宮」と称されたが（『{{jalink|藤川記}}』5月24日条に「水口を過くとて（中略）新宮の馬場に至る、（中略）新宮は山王にてましますとかや」とある。ちなみに、『藤川記』は{{jalink|応仁の乱|Q385676}}により{{jalink|奈良|Q11444975}}に疎開していた{{jalink|一条兼良|Q1656375}}が、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}5年（{{jalink|1473年|Q6594}}）に{{jalink|美濃国|Q864156|美濃}}の{{jalink|守護代|Q568360}}{{jalink|斎藤妙椿|Q3469318}}の招きに応じて同国へ下向し、奈良へ帰還するまでの1ヶ月間の紀行文である。）、近世には「大宮大明神社」と称され（『{{jalink|近江輿地志略|Q85883966}}』）、現在でも土地では「大宮さん」と称されている。 === 祭神 === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|本殿]] * 主祭神 - {{jalink|大水口宿禰|Q11436785|大水口宿禰命}}（おおみなくちのすくねのみこと） * 相殿神 - {{jalink|大己貴命}}、{{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|素盞嗚尊}}、{{jalink|稲田姫命}} 大水口宿禰命は、{{jalink|物部氏|Q1755836}}の同族である{{jalink|穂積氏|Q11597079|穂積臣}}等の祖とされる人物であるが『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』垂仁天皇25年3月一云条、『{{jalink|新撰姓氏録|Q1045362|姓氏録}}』{{jalink|左京|Q11479660}}{{jalink|神別|Q11588768}}穂積臣条など。、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|延喜神名式}}』の{{jalink|甲賀郡|Q4227244}}に「川枯神社二座現在甲賀市水口町嶬峨1607-1の{{jalink|八坂神社 (甲賀市)|Q72183108|八坂神社}}が比定されている。」とある「'''川枯神'''（かはかれのかみ）」が『{{jalink|旧事本紀}}』天孫本紀の物部氏系図に載せる大水口宿禰命の祖母、'''淡海川枯姫'''（あはみのかはかれひめ）に関係すると考え、当地に蟠踞した物部氏の一族が祖先を祀ったものであろうとされている『{{jalink|大日本史|Q1156003|大日本史神祇志}}』、『{{jalink|延喜式神名御秘抄}}』、{{jalink|伴信友|Q4346747}}『{{jalink|神名帳考証土代|Q11588823}}』、{{jalink|鈴鹿連胤|Q6536446}}『{{jalink|神社覈録|Q11590708}}』など。。また鎮座地一帯は旧甲賀郡の中心部に位置し、周辺には郡内最大規模の{{jalink|波濤が平古墳}}といった{{jalink|古墳|Q1141225|後期古墳}}も点在することから、古くより開かれた地であることが判るので、神社名や祭神名から見て、早くから灌漑用水を司る農耕の神としての役割も担っていたと推考されている『日本の神々』、昭和61年（満田良順「水口神社 川田神社」）。。 なお、現在相殿とされている3柱は{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}の初年（18世紀初）には主祭神とされていたこともあるが社家の石王家代々の日記である『石王氏日記』に見える。、中世に当神社が近接する{{jalink|天台宗|Q668188}}の名刹{{jalink|大岡寺|Q60988301}}の{{jalink|護法神}}とされていることから{{jalink|江間氏親}}『{{jalink|行嚢抄}}』、及び前掲『神名帳考証土代』。、{{jalink|延暦寺|Q917195}}の護法神である{{jalink|日吉大社|Q656451|日吉社}}西本宮の大己貴命を{{jalink|勧請}}し、更にその親神とされる2柱を追加したものと考えられている『式内社調査報告』、昭和56年（宇野茂樹「水口神社」）。。 === 歴史 === 社伝によれば、{{jalink|垂仁天皇|Q314850}}の時代に{{jalink|クシナダヒメ|Q505469|稲田姫命}}が{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602}}の神鏡を奉じて{{jalink|近江国|Q843882|近江}}に入国、族長であった{{jalink|矢田部氏||矢田部宿祢}}蔵田麿（やたべのすくねくらたまろ）に神鏡を鎮座させるべき地を問うたところ{{jalink|甲可日雲宮}}（こうがのひくものみや）の地が最適であると回答したため、甲可川（現{{jalink|野洲川|Q8049935}}）を遡上して現社地に至り、4年間神鏡を奉じるとともに大己貴命を神鏡守護の神として祀ったのに創まるというが、これは{{jalink|倭姫命|Q4388732}}が天照大神の神鏡を奉じて各地を巡幸したという{{jalink|伊勢神宮|Q687168|伊勢の神宮}}の鎮座伝承の「倭姫命」を「稲田姫命」に変えたもので、甲賀郡には古くから{{jalink|伊勢国|Q864166|伊勢}}への{{jalink|奉幣使}}が利用する'''伊勢大路'''が通るなど密な関係を有していたため、後世に神宮の{{jalink|御師|Q11488972}}などの唱導活動によってもたらされた『{{jalink|倭姫命世記|Q131331326}}』を直接の下敷きにして『倭姫命世記』垂仁天皇4年条に「遷淡海国甲可日雲宮四年奉斎、于時淡海国造進地口御田」とあり、甲賀郡での4年間の奉斎を伝えるが、この甲可日雲宮の伝承地も水口町一帯に存在する。詳しくは{{jalink|元伊勢#一覧|Q11387775|元伊勢}}の「近江国甲可日雲宮」参照。造作されたものであろうと考えられている。そのため、これに対して上述のように本来は当地の物部氏の一族が祖先を奉斎したのが創祀であると見られている（社伝に見える矢田部氏も物部氏の同族である『{{jalink|新撰姓氏録|Q1045362}}』（左京神別上・大和国神別等）、『旧事本紀』天孫本紀。）。 『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}元年（{{jalink|859年|Q23897}}）1月27日条に従五位下から従五位上に昇叙されたことが見え、{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|延喜の制}}で国幣小社に列した。その他の史料を欠くが、上述したように中世には{{jalink|大岡寺|Q60988301}}の護法神として「山王新宮」と称され、社伝によると東西30町、南北25町といった広大な境内地を有し、{{jalink|永禄|Q1143341}}年間（{{jalink|1558年|Q6601}} - {{jalink|1569年|Q6653}}）にその大半を喪失、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}年間（{{jalink|1573年|Q6678}} - {{jalink|1593年|Q6772}}）には130石あった{{jalink|社領}}も没収、以後4間から5間四方といった狭隘な境内地を有するのみになったという。{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}3年（{{jalink|1663年|Q7577}}）に{{jalink|江戸幕府|Q205662|幕府}}の代官小堀仁右衛門が郷民に命じて社殿を再建させ、{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}10年（{{jalink|1697年|Q7702}}）にも拝殿が修復された記録があるが、{{jalink|正徳 (日本)|Q1193848|正徳}}5年（{{jalink|1715年|Q6884}}）に{{jalink|水口藩|Q10863504|水口藩主}}として帰封された{{jalink|加藤嘉矩|Q11399105}}が初入国をした時に、当神社の荒廃を嘆いて町民に社殿を造営させ、{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}14年（{{jalink|1729年|Q6952}}）9月に{{jalink|遷宮|Q2271023}}を行ったといい、以来藩制時代を通じて{{jalink|加藤氏|Q10900894}}の保護を受けた。甲賀郡は古代には上述伊勢大路が通い、{{jalink|斎宮|Q150845|斎内親王}}の群行のための{{jalink|垂水頓宮}}が置かれたりした『三代実録』{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年（{{jalink|886年|Q24683}}）9月30日条。ほか、甲賀駅が設けられて{{jalink|駅馬|Q65273644}}を常置させる（『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448|延喜兵部式}}』）など、{{jalink|東海道|Q1196306}}の要衝ともされた地で、{{jalink|水口宿|Q3069768|水口}}にも中世以来{{jalink|宿駅}}が置かれていたが、加藤氏の帰封以来、その{{jalink|城下町|Q909458}}として、また{{jalink|東海道五十三次|Q3134262}}の{{jalink|宿場町}}として栄え、それにともなって当神社も大いに繁栄した。 {{jalink|1876年|Q7766}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}9年）10月に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|郷社}}に列し、{{jalink|1894年|Q7843}}（明治27年）境内を拡張、それとともに社殿造営にも着手して{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}（明治30年）に竣工、{{jalink|1924年|Q2173}}（{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}13年）6月には県社に昇格した。{{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}後は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}に属している。 === 神職 === 近世を通じて石王家が{{jalink|社家|Q11588367}}として宮司職を襲っている。石王氏は本姓矢田部氏を称すが、北内貴の土豪である{{jalink|内貴氏}}の一族であったともいわれている『滋賀県の地名』、平成3年（「水口神社」）。 === 祭祀 === [[File:水口曳山祭.jpg|thumb|水口曳山祭]] {{main|水口曳山祭}} 現在の甲賀市水口町本町、同市水口町松栄、同市水口町鹿深といった水口地区の中心部を氏子区域とするが、これらは近世には東の水口と西の美濃部の2地区に2分され、それぞれの地区に{{jalink|宮座|Q11454763}}が組織されていた。水口地区では年老30人が選出されて大宮（本社）へ奉仕するとともに、神殿（こうどの）と称する1人が神事を司った。美濃部地区では摂社御武社（現武雄神社）を美濃部の氏神と定め、年老10人と水口同様1人の神殿を選出して神事を司らしめた。また{{jalink|野洲川|Q8049935}}を隔てて南接する北内貴も氏子区域とされて宮座を組織していたが、これは中世に遡って北内貴が水口美濃部部落と1つの郷を形成していたためであると考えられている。これらの宮座は明治初年まで存続していたが、{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}（明治6年）に廃止された前掲『石王氏日記』明治6年条に「大宮村四座と武雄一座、内貴一座を廃す」とある。。 当神社の代表的な神事は{{jalink|4月20日|Q2524}}の例祭'''水口祭'''である。祭日は古く4月の上申日とされており、これは「山王新宮」と称された頃から日吉社の神使である{{jalink|猿}}に因んで設定されたものとされるが、明治以降{{jalink|グレゴリオ暦|Q12138|太陽暦}}へ改めるとともに祭式も改変し、{{jalink|1896年|Q7846}}（明治29年）に現行日とされた。当日には町内から{{jalink|曳山}}が奉納されるため、'''{{jalink|水口曳山祭|Q11548378}}'''とも称され、県指定{{jalink|無形民俗文化財}}となっている。 また、{{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}}の頃（17世紀後半）から旱魃に対する{{jalink|雨乞い|Q477738|雨乞祭}}が行われるようになり、その折には藩主の親拝、または代参があったという滋賀県神社庁ウェブサイトより。。 === 境内 === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|鳥居]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|境内]] * 本殿 - {{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}30年）再建。一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}。 * 祝詞舎 * 武雄神社 - 祭神：{{jalink|武甕槌神}}・{{jalink|経津主神|Q1107286}}。上述のように近世には美濃部の氏神とされ、西部の宮座が神事等を担当していた。 * 玉津米神社 - 祭神：玉留産霊尊 * 水口{{jalink|恵比須神社}} - 祭神：{{jalink|事代主神}} * {{jalink|日枝神社|Q4189425}} - 祭神：{{jalink|大山咋神|Q10935105}}・{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}} * 中門 * 拝殿 - {{jalink|弘化|Q1325317}}元年（{{jalink|1844年|Q7633}}）再建。{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}}。 * 絵馬殿 - 境内に絵馬殿を有する神社は近江に少なく、貴重な例となっている。 * {{jalink|稲荷神社|Q514480}} * 社務所 * 太鼓橋 === 文化財 === === 重要文化財 === * 木造女神坐像（じょしんざぞう） - 像高22.2cmの彩色像像高は毎日新聞社『国宝・重要文化財大全』、久野健編『図説仏像巡礼事典』（新訂版、山川出版社、1994）による。。垂髪で両手を袖に入れ、胸先で拱手する。着衣の衣褶を彫らず、膝前も造作していない素朴な作風の像である。{{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}の作と推定される。{{jalink|1911年|Q2076}}（明治44年）8月9日に{{jalink|古社寺保存法|Q11411552}}に基づき国宝（旧国宝）に指定、{{jalink|1950年|Q18597}}（{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}25年）8月の{{jalink|文化財保護法|Q1188502}}施行により{{jalink|重要文化財|Q1188622}}となっている。 === 滋賀県指定無形民俗文化財 === * 水口曳山祭（水口祭保存振興会） === 滋賀県選択無形民俗文化財 === * 水口曳山祭（水口祭曳山保存会） === 甲賀市指定有形文化財 === * 石灯籠 - {{jalink|南北朝時代 (日本)|Q264012|南北朝時代}}の{{jalink|康永|Q980266}}元年（{{jalink|1342年|Q6069}}）の刻銘があり、製作年代が判明する。 === その他 === * かつて社家の石王家に「水口薬」という薬が家伝として伝わっており、{{jalink|1882年|Q7812}}（明治15年）には薬名「安体散」として{{jalink|内務省 (日本)|Q1142265|内務省}}の認可も受け、『{{jalink|大日本地名辞書|Q17213149}}』にも『{{jalink|大同類聚方|Q11433347}}』を引いて「水口薬、近江国水口神社祝等之家伝、元者大己貴神力也、熱去寒来、日々時々渇、而腸内動気効云々」と記しているが、現在は伝えられていない。 * 社家石王家の日記に、{{jalink|1868年|Q7717}}（明治元年）9月、天皇東幸の折に{{jalink|勅使|Q11399920}}として{{jalink|植松雅言|Q22128646}}を当神社に差遣したことが見える。 === 交通アクセス === * {{jalink|近江鉄道|Q3276327}}{{jalink|水口城南駅|Q3184422}}から徒歩10分 === 脚注 === link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 水口神社 |画像 = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地= {{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|甲賀市|Q847584}}{{jalink|水口町|Q3314706}}宮の前3-14 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=水口神社 (甲賀市)}} |祭神 = 大水口宿禰命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 伝・{{jalink|垂仁天皇|Q314850}}朝 |本殿 = 一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |例祭 = {{jalink|4月20日|Q2524}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=水口神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''水口神社'''（みなくちじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|滋賀県|Q131358}}{{jalink|甲賀市|Q847584}}{{jalink|水口町|Q3314706}}宮の前にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}。旧社格は{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|県社}}。例祭として{{jalink|水口曳山祭|Q11548378}}を行う。 === 社名 === 中世には「山王新宮」と称されたが（『{{jalink|藤川記}}』5月24日条に「水口を過くとて（中略）新宮の馬場に至る、（中略）新宮は山王にてましますとかや」とある。ちなみに、『藤川記』は{{jalink|応仁の乱|Q385676}}により{{jalink|奈良|Q11444975}}に疎開していた{{jalink|一条兼良|Q1656375}}が、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}5年（{{jalink|1473年|Q6594}}）に{{jalink|美濃国|Q864156|美濃}}の{{jalink|守護代|Q568360}}{{jalink|斎藤妙椿|Q3469318}}の招きに応じて同国へ下向し、奈良へ帰還するまでの1ヶ月間の紀行文である。）、近世には「大宮大明神社」と称され（『{{jalink|近江輿地志略|Q85883966}}』）、現在でも土地では「大宮さん」と称されている。 === 祭神 === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|本殿]] * 主祭神 - {{jalink|大水口宿禰|Q11436785|大水口宿禰命}}（おおみなくちのすくねのみこと） * 相殿神 - {{jalink|大己貴命}}、{{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|素盞嗚尊}}、{{jalink|稲田姫命}} 大水口宿禰命は、{{jalink|物部氏|Q1755836}}の同族である{{jalink|穂積氏|Q11597079|穂積臣}}等の祖とされる人物であるが『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』垂仁天皇25年3月一云条、『{{jalink|新撰姓氏録|Q1045362|姓氏録}}』{{jalink|左京|Q11479660}}{{jalink|神別|Q11588768}}穂積臣条など。、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|延喜神名式}}』の{{jalink|甲賀郡|Q4227244}}に「川枯神社二座現在甲賀市水口町嶬峨1607-1の{{jalink|八坂神社 (甲賀市)|Q72183108|八坂神社}}が比定されている。」とある「'''川枯神'''（かはかれのかみ）」が『{{jalink|旧事本紀}}』天孫本紀の物部氏系図に載せる大水口宿禰命の祖母、'''淡海川枯姫'''（あはみのかはかれひめ）に関係すると考え、当地に蟠踞した物部氏の一族が祖先を祀ったものであろうとされている『{{jalink|大日本史|Q1156003|大日本史神祇志}}』、『{{jalink|延喜式神名御秘抄}}』、{{jalink|伴信友|Q4346747}}『{{jalink|神名帳考証土代|Q11588823}}』、{{jalink|鈴鹿連胤|Q6536446}}『{{jalink|神社覈録|Q11590708}}』など。。また鎮座地一帯は旧甲賀郡の中心部に位置し、周辺には郡内最大規模の{{jalink|波濤が平古墳}}といった{{jalink|古墳|Q1141225|後期古墳}}も点在することから、古くより開かれた地であることが判るので、神社名や祭神名から見て、早くから灌漑用水を司る農耕の神としての役割も担っていたと推考されている『日本の神々』、昭和61年（満田良順「水口神社 川田神社」）。。 なお、現在相殿とされている3柱は{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}の初年（18世紀初）には主祭神とされていたこともあるが社家の石王家代々の日記である『石王氏日記』に見える。、中世に当神社が近接する{{jalink|天台宗|Q668188}}の名刹{{jalink|大岡寺|Q60988301}}の{{jalink|護法神}}とされていることから{{jalink|江間氏親}}『{{jalink|行嚢抄}}』、及び前掲『神名帳考証土代』。、{{jalink|延暦寺|Q917195}}の護法神である{{jalink|日吉大社|Q656451|日吉社}}西本宮の大己貴命を{{jalink|勧請}}し、更にその親神とされる2柱を追加したものと考えられている『式内社調査報告』、昭和56年（宇野茂樹「水口神社」）。。 === 歴史 === 社伝によれば、{{jalink|垂仁天皇|Q314850}}の時代に{{jalink|クシナダヒメ|Q505469|稲田姫命}}が{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602}}の神鏡を奉じて{{jalink|近江国|Q843882|近江}}に入国、族長であった{{jalink|矢田部氏||矢田部宿祢}}蔵田麿（やたべのすくねくらたまろ）に神鏡を鎮座させるべき地を問うたところ{{jalink|甲可日雲宮}}（こうがのひくものみや）の地が最適であると回答したため、甲可川（現{{jalink|野洲川|Q8049935}}）を遡上して現社地に至り、4年間神鏡を奉じるとともに大己貴命を神鏡守護の神として祀ったのに創まるというが、これは{{jalink|倭姫命|Q4388732}}が天照大神の神鏡を奉じて各地を巡幸したという{{jalink|伊勢神宮|Q687168|伊勢の神宮}}の鎮座伝承の「倭姫命」を「稲田姫命」に変えたもので、甲賀郡には古くから{{jalink|伊勢国|Q864166|伊勢}}への{{jalink|奉幣使}}が利用する'''伊勢大路'''が通るなど密な関係を有していたため、後世に神宮の{{jalink|御師|Q11488972}}などの唱導活動によってもたらされた『{{jalink|倭姫命世記|Q131331326}}』を直接の下敷きにして『倭姫命世記』垂仁天皇4年条に「遷淡海国甲可日雲宮四年奉斎、于時淡海国造進地口御田」とあり、甲賀郡での4年間の奉斎を伝えるが、この甲可日雲宮の伝承地も水口町一帯に存在する。詳しくは{{jalink|元伊勢#一覧|Q11387775|元伊勢}}の「近江国甲可日雲宮」参照。造作されたものであろうと考えられている。そのため、これに対して上述のように本来は当地の物部氏の一族が祖先を奉斎したのが創祀であると見られている（社伝に見える矢田部氏も物部氏の同族である『{{jalink|新撰姓氏録|Q1045362}}』（左京神別上・大和国神別等）、『旧事本紀』天孫本紀。）。 『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}元年（{{jalink|859年|Q23897}}）1月27日条に従五位下から従五位上に昇叙されたことが見え、{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|延喜の制}}で国幣小社に列した。その他の史料を欠くが、上述したように中世には{{jalink|大岡寺|Q60988301}}の護法神として「山王新宮」と称され、社伝によると東西30町、南北25町といった広大な境内地を有し、{{jalink|永禄|Q1143341}}年間（{{jalink|1558年|Q6601}} - {{jalink|1569年|Q6653}}）にその大半を喪失、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}年間（{{jalink|1573年|Q6678}} - {{jalink|1593年|Q6772}}）には130石あった{{jalink|社領}}も没収、以後4間から5間四方といった狭隘な境内地を有するのみになったという。{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}3年（{{jalink|1663年|Q7577}}）に{{jalink|江戸幕府|Q205662|幕府}}の代官小堀仁右衛門が郷民に命じて社殿を再建させ、{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}10年（{{jalink|1697年|Q7702}}）にも拝殿が修復された記録があるが、{{jalink|正徳 (日本)|Q1193848|正徳}}5年（{{jalink|1715年|Q6884}}）に{{jalink|水口藩|Q10863504|水口藩主}}として帰封された{{jalink|加藤嘉矩|Q11399105}}が初入国をした時に、当神社の荒廃を嘆いて町民に社殿を造営させ、{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}14年（{{jalink|1729年|Q6952}}）9月に{{jalink|遷宮|Q2271023}}を行ったといい、以来藩制時代を通じて{{jalink|加藤氏|Q10900894}}の保護を受けた。甲賀郡は古代には上述伊勢大路が通い、{{jalink|斎宮|Q150845|斎内親王}}の群行のための{{jalink|垂水頓宮}}が置かれたりした『三代実録』{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年（{{jalink|886年|Q24683}}）9月30日条。ほか、甲賀駅が設けられて{{jalink|駅馬|Q65273644}}を常置させる（『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448|延喜兵部式}}』）など、{{jalink|東海道|Q1196306}}の要衝ともされた地で、{{jalink|水口宿|Q3069768|水口}}にも中世以来{{jalink|宿駅}}が置かれていたが、加藤氏の帰封以来、その{{jalink|城下町|Q909458}}として、また{{jalink|東海道五十三次|Q3134262}}の{{jalink|宿場町}}として栄え、それにともなって当神社も大いに繁栄した。 {{jalink|1876年|Q7766}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}9年）10月に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|郷社}}に列し、{{jalink|1894年|Q7843}}（明治27年）境内を拡張、それとともに社殿造営にも着手して{{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}（明治30年）に竣工、{{jalink|1924年|Q2173}}（{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}13年）6月には県社に昇格した。{{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}後は{{jalink|神社本庁|Q908077}}に属している。 === 神職 === 近世を通じて石王家が{{jalink|社家|Q11588367}}として宮司職を襲っている。石王氏は本姓矢田部氏を称すが、北内貴の土豪である{{jalink|内貴氏}}の一族であったともいわれている『滋賀県の地名』、平成3年（「水口神社」）。 === 祭祀 === [[File:水口曳山祭.jpg|thumb|水口曳山祭]] {{main|水口曳山祭}} 現在の甲賀市水口町本町、同市水口町松栄、同市水口町鹿深といった水口地区の中心部を氏子区域とするが、これらは近世には東の水口と西の美濃部の2地区に2分され、それぞれの地区に{{jalink|宮座|Q11454763}}が組織されていた。水口地区では年老30人が選出されて大宮（本社）へ奉仕するとともに、神殿（こうどの）と称する1人が神事を司った。美濃部地区では摂社御武社（現武雄神社）を美濃部の氏神と定め、年老10人と水口同様1人の神殿を選出して神事を司らしめた。また{{jalink|野洲川|Q8049935}}を隔てて南接する北内貴も氏子区域とされて宮座を組織していたが、これは中世に遡って北内貴が水口美濃部部落と1つの郷を形成していたためであると考えられている。これらの宮座は明治初年まで存続していたが、{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}（明治6年）に廃止された前掲『石王氏日記』明治6年条に「大宮村四座と武雄一座、内貴一座を廃す」とある。。 当神社の代表的な神事は{{jalink|4月20日|Q2524}}の例祭'''水口祭'''である。祭日は古く4月の上申日とされており、これは「山王新宮」と称された頃から日吉社の神使である{{jalink|猿}}に因んで設定されたものとされるが、明治以降{{jalink|グレゴリオ暦|Q12138|太陽暦}}へ改めるとともに祭式も改変し、{{jalink|1896年|Q7846}}（明治29年）に現行日とされた。当日には町内から{{jalink|曳山}}が奉納されるため、'''{{jalink|水口曳山祭|Q11548378}}'''とも称され、県指定{{jalink|無形民俗文化財}}となっている。 また、{{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}}の頃（17世紀後半）から旱魃に対する{{jalink|雨乞い|Q477738|雨乞祭}}が行われるようになり、その折には藩主の親拝、または代参があったという滋賀県神社庁ウェブサイトより。。 === 境内 === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|鳥居]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|境内]] * 本殿 - {{jalink|1897年|Q7847}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}30年）再建。一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}。 * 祝詞舎 * 武雄神社 - 祭神：{{jalink|武甕槌神}}・{{jalink|経津主神|Q1107286}}。上述のように近世には美濃部の氏神とされ、西部の宮座が神事等を担当していた。 * 玉津米神社 - 祭神：玉留産霊尊 * 水口{{jalink|恵比須神社}} - 祭神：{{jalink|事代主神}} * {{jalink|日枝神社|Q4189425}} - 祭神：{{jalink|大山咋神|Q10935105}}・{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}} * 中門 * 拝殿 - {{jalink|弘化|Q1325317}}元年（{{jalink|1844年|Q7633}}）再建。{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}}。 * 絵馬殿 - 境内に絵馬殿を有する神社は近江に少なく、貴重な例となっている。 * {{jalink|稲荷神社|Q514480}} * 社務所 * 太鼓橋 === 文化財 === === 重要文化財 === * 木造女神坐像（じょしんざぞう） - 像高22.2cmの彩色像像高は毎日新聞社『国宝・重要文化財大全』、久野健編『図説仏像巡礼事典』（新訂版、山川出版社、1994）による。。垂髪で両手を袖に入れ、胸先で拱手する。着衣の衣褶を彫らず、膝前も造作していない素朴な作風の像である。{{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}の作と推定される。{{jalink|1911年|Q2076}}（明治44年）8月9日に{{jalink|古社寺保存法|Q11411552}}に基づき国宝（旧国宝）に指定、{{jalink|1950年|Q18597}}（{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}25年）8月の{{jalink|文化財保護法|Q1188502}}施行により{{jalink|重要文化財|Q1188622}}となっている。 === 滋賀県指定無形民俗文化財 === * 水口曳山祭（水口祭保存振興会） === 滋賀県選択無形民俗文化財 === * 水口曳山祭（水口祭曳山保存会） === 甲賀市指定有形文化財 === * 石灯籠 - {{jalink|南北朝時代 (日本)|Q264012|南北朝時代}}の{{jalink|康永|Q980266}}元年（{{jalink|1342年|Q6069}}）の刻銘があり、製作年代が判明する。 === その他 === * かつて社家の石王家に「水口薬」という薬が家伝として伝わっており、{{jalink|1882年|Q7812}}（明治15年）には薬名「安体散」として{{jalink|内務省 (日本)|Q1142265|内務省}}の認可も受け、『{{jalink|大日本地名辞書|Q17213149}}』にも『{{jalink|大同類聚方|Q11433347}}』を引いて「水口薬、近江国水口神社祝等之家伝、元者大己貴神力也、熱去寒来、日々時々渇、而腸内動気効云々」と記しているが、現在は伝えられていない。 * 社家石王家の日記に、{{jalink|1868年|Q7717}}（明治元年）9月、天皇東幸の折に{{jalink|勅使|Q11399920}}として{{jalink|植松雅言|Q22128646}}を当神社に差遣したことが見える。 === 交通アクセス === * {{jalink|近江鉄道|Q3276327}}{{jalink|水口城南駅|Q3184422}}から徒歩10分 === 脚注 === link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizuguchi Shrine |Image = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Koka City|Q847584}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706}}Miyanomae 3-14 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees, minutes, seconds|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizuguchi Shrine (Koka City)}} |Enshrined deity = Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small)
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = Legend・{{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} morning |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Regular festival = {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Mizuguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minakuchi Shrine'' (Minakuchi Shrine) is located in front of the shrine in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Koka City|Q847584}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}}. The old company rank is {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Prefectural company}}. The annual festival is the {{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{jalink|Fujikawa}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{jalink|Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375}} who was evacuated to {{jalink|Nara|Q11444975}} due to the {{jalink|Onin War|Q385676}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{jalink|1473|Q6594}}) of {{jalink|Mino Province|Q864156|Mino}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{jalink|Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{jalink|Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{jalink|Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{jalink|Otomi Takamikoto}}, {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Sojinson}}, {{jalink|Inada Hime no Mikoto}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Hozumi clan|Q11597079|Hozumiomi}}, who is a member of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|Surnames and Names and Records}}' {{jalink|Sakyo|Q11479660}}{{jalink|Kamibetsu|Q11588768}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engishi Shinmyoki}}'s {{jalink|Koga District|Q4227244}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|Yasaka Shrine}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{jalink|Kuji Honki}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{jalink|Great Japanese History|Q1156003|Great Japanese History Jingishi}}", "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho}}", {{jalink|Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747}} ``{{jalink|Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823}}'', {{jalink|Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446}}, ``{{jalink|Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{jalink|Hatogahira Tumulus}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{jalink|guardian deity}} of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, a famous temple of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{jalink|Kansei}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Shrine}}, which is the guardian deity of {{jalink|Enryakuji|Q917195}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime|Q505469|Inadahime no Mikoto}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882|Omi}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{jalink|Yatabe clan||Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{jalink|Kokahikumogu}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|Ise Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{jalink|envoys}} to {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} Directly based on "{{jalink|Wahime no Miki|Q131331326}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{jalink|Motoise#List|Q11387775|Motoise}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{jalink|Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{jalink|Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engi system}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}} ({{jalink|1558|Q6601}} - Most of it was lost in {{jalink|1569|Q6653}}, and for {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}} - In {{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the {{jalink|sha territory}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1663|Q7577}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{jalink|Edo Shogunate|Q205662|Bakufu}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}10 ({{jalink|1697|Q7702}}), but {{jalink|Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|Mashinori}}5 years ({{jalink|1715|Q6884}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{jalink|Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 14 ({{jalink|1729|Q6952}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{jalink|Kato clan|Q10900894}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{jalink|Tarumitongu}} was located for the group of {{jalink|Saikū|Q150845|Prince Sainai}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{jalink|Ninwa|Q1346317}} 2nd year ({{jalink|886|Q24683}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{jalink|Ekima|Q65273644}} was permanently stationed there (``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448|Engishiki|Engishiki|Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{jalink|Tokaido|Q1196306}}.{{jalink|Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768| Mizuguchi}} has also had a {{jalink|shuku station}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{jalink|castle town|Q909458}} and also as a {{jalink|post town}} of {{jalink|53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9), it was listed as {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|Gosha}}, and in {{jalink|1894|Q7843}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, it belongs to {{jalink|Shrine Headquarters|Q908077}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{jalink|Shake|Q11588367}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{jalink|Uchitaka clan}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{jalink|Miyaza|Q11454763}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{jalink|Yasu River|Q8049935}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{jalink|monkey}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138|solar calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{jalink|1896|Q7846}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{jalink|Hikiyama}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}. Also, from around the {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} period (late 17th century), {{jalink|rain praying|Q477738|rain praying festival}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}30). Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Takemikazuchijin}}, {{jalink|Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{jalink|Ebisu Shrine}} - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Kotoshiro Nushikami}} * {{jalink|Hie Shrine|Q4189425}} - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105}}・{{jalink|Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{jalink|Kohka|Q1325317}} ({{jalink|1844|Q7633}}). {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{jalink|shadow|Q8364380}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. {{jalink|1911|Q2076}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{jalink|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552}}, {{jalink|1950|Q185 97}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{jalink|Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502}}, it became {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} ({{jalink|1342|Q6069}}) of the {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{jalink|1882|Q7812}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{jalink|Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149}}" also includes "{{jalink|Daidoruijuhou|Q114 33347}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}} sent {{jalink|Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{jalink|Ohmi Railway|Q3276327}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{jalink|Kogakkan University}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{jalink|Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535}} supervised by {{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{jalink|Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}, 1945) *{{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites" Volume 5 Yamashiro and Omi [Reprinted], {{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02505-5 (first edition was published in 1986 ISBN) 4-560-02215-1) *"Place Names of Shiga Prefecture" (Japan Historical Place Names), Heibonsha, 1991 ISBN 4-582-49025-5 === External link === {{Commonscat|Minakuchi-jinja (Koka)|Mizuguchi Shrine}} * {{official|https://minakuchi-jinja.com/}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=547 Mizuguchi Shrine] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Minakuchishinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:甲賀市の歴史]] [[Category:甲賀市の建築物]] [[Category:甲賀市の重要文化財]] [[Category:水口藩]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizuguchi Shrine |Image = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location= {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Koka City|Q847584}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706}}Miyanomae 3-14 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees, minutes, seconds|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizuguchi Shrine (Koka City)}} |Enshrined deity = Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small)
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = Legend・{{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} morning |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Regular festival = {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Mizuguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minakuchi Shrine'' (Minakuchi Shrine) is located in front of the shrine in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Koka City|Q847584}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}}. The old company rank is {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Prefectural company}}. The annual festival is the {{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{jalink|Fujikawa}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{jalink|Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375}} who was evacuated to {{jalink|Nara|Q11444975}} due to the {{jalink|Onin War|Q385676}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{jalink|1473|Q6594}}) of {{jalink|Mino Province|Q864156|Mino}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{jalink|Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{jalink|Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{jalink|Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{jalink|Otomi Takamikoto}}, {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Sojinson}}, {{jalink|Inada Hime no Mikoto}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Hozumi clan|Q11597079|Hozumiomi}}, who is a member of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|Surnames and Names and Records}}' {{jalink|Sakyo|Q11479660}}{{jalink|Kamibetsu|Q11588768}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engishi Shinmyoki}}'s {{jalink|Koga District|Q4227244}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|Yasaka Shrine}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{jalink|Kuji Honki}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{jalink|Great Japanese History|Q1156003|Great Japanese History Jingishi}}", "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho}}", {{jalink|Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747}} ``{{jalink|Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823}}'', {{jalink|Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446}}, ``{{jalink|Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{jalink|Hatogahira Tumulus}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{jalink|guardian deity}} of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, a famous temple of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{jalink|Kansei}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Shrine}}, which is the guardian deity of {{jalink|Enryakuji|Q917195}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime|Q505469|Inadahime no Mikoto}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882|Omi}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{jalink|Yatabe clan||Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{jalink|Kokahikumogu}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|Ise Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{jalink|envoys}} to {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} Directly based on "{{jalink|Wahime no Miki|Q131331326}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{jalink|Motoise#List|Q11387775|Motoise}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{jalink|Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{jalink|Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engi system}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}} ({{jalink|1558|Q6601}} - Most of it was lost in {{jalink|1569|Q6653}}, and for {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}} - In {{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the {{jalink|sha territory}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1663|Q7577}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{jalink|Edo Shogunate|Q205662|Bakufu}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}10 ({{jalink|1697|Q7702}}), but {{jalink|Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|Mashinori}}5 years ({{jalink|1715|Q6884}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{jalink|Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 14 ({{jalink|1729|Q6952}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{jalink|Kato clan|Q10900894}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{jalink|Tarumitongu}} was located for the group of {{jalink|Saikū|Q150845|Prince Sainai}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{jalink|Ninwa|Q1346317}} 2nd year ({{jalink|886|Q24683}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{jalink|Ekima|Q65273644}} was permanently stationed there (``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448|Engishiki|Engishiki|Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{jalink|Tokaido|Q1196306}}.{{jalink|Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768| Mizuguchi}} has also had a {{jalink|shuku station}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{jalink|castle town|Q909458}} and also as a {{jalink|post town}} of {{jalink|53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9), it was listed as {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|Gosha}}, and in {{jalink|1894|Q7843}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, it belongs to {{jalink|Shrine Headquarters|Q908077}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{jalink|Shake|Q11588367}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{jalink|Uchitaka clan}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{jalink|Miyaza|Q11454763}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{jalink|Yasu River|Q8049935}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{jalink|monkey}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138|solar calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{jalink|1896|Q7846}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{jalink|Hikiyama}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}. Also, from around the {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} period (late 17th century), {{jalink|rain praying|Q477738|rain praying festival}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}30). Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Takemikazuchijin}}, {{jalink|Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{jalink|Ebisu Shrine}} - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Kotoshiro Nushikami}} * {{jalink|Hie Shrine|Q4189425}} - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105}}・{{jalink|Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{jalink|Kohka|Q1325317}} ({{jalink|1844|Q7633}}). {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{jalink|shadow|Q8364380}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. {{jalink|1911|Q2076}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{jalink|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552}}, {{jalink|1950|Q185 97}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{jalink|Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502}}, it became {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} ({{jalink|1342|Q6069}}) of the {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{jalink|1882|Q7812}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{jalink|Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149}}" also includes "{{jalink|Daidoruijuhou|Q114 33347}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}} sent {{jalink|Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{jalink|Ohmi Railway|Q3276327}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{jalink|Kogakkan University}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{jalink|Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535}} supervised by {{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{jalink|Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}, 1945) *{{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites" Volume 5 Yamashiro and Omi [Reprinted], {{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02505-5 (first edition was published in 1986 ISBN) 4-560-02215-1) *"Place Names of Shiga Prefecture" (Japan Historical Place Names), Heibonsha, 1991 ISBN 4-582-49025-5 === External link === {{Commonscat|Minakuchi-jinja (Koka)|Mizuguchi Shrine}} * {{official|https://minakuchi-jinja.com/}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=547 Mizuguchi Shrine] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Minakuchishinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:甲賀市の歴史]] [[Category:甲賀市の建築物]] [[Category:甲賀市の重要文化財]] [[Category:水口藩]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = Legend・{{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}} morning |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Regular festival = {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Mizuguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minakuchi Shrine'' (Minakuchi Shrine) is located in front of the shrine in {{jalink|Shiga Prefecture|Q131358}}{{jalink|Koka City|Q847584}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}}. The old company rank is {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Prefectural company}}. The annual festival is the {{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{jalink|Fujikawa}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{jalink|Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375}} who was evacuated to {{jalink|Nara|Q11444975}} due to the {{jalink|Onin War|Q385676}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{jalink|1473|Q6594}}) of {{jalink|Mino Province|Q864156|Mino}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{jalink|Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{jalink|Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{jalink|Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{jalink|Otomi Takamikoto}}, {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Sojinson}}, {{jalink|Inada Hime no Mikoto}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{jalink|Hozumi clan|Q11597079|Hozumiomi}}, who is a member of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|Surnames and Names and Records}}' {{jalink|Sakyo|Q11479660}}{{jalink|Kamibetsu|Q11588768}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engishi Shinmyoki}}'s {{jalink|Koga District|Q4227244}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|Yasaka Shrine}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{jalink|Kuji Honki}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{jalink|Great Japanese History|Q1156003|Great Japanese History Jingishi}}", "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho}}", {{jalink|Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747}} ``{{jalink|Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823}}'', {{jalink|Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446}}, ``{{jalink|Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{jalink|Hatogahira Tumulus}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{jalink|guardian deity}} of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, a famous temple of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{jalink|Kansei}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{jalink|Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|Hiyoshi Shrine}}, which is the guardian deity of {{jalink|Enryakuji|Q917195}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime|Q505469|Inadahime no Mikoto}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{jalink|Omi Province|Q843882|Omi}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{jalink|Yatabe clan||Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{jalink|Kokahikumogu}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|Ise Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{jalink|envoys}} to {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166|Ise}} Directly based on "{{jalink|Wahime no Miki|Q131331326}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{jalink|Motoise#List|Q11387775|Motoise}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{jalink|Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{jalink|Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981}}" {{jalink|Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Engi system}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{jalink|Ookaji|Q60988301}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}} ({{jalink|1558|Q6601}} - Most of it was lost in {{jalink|1569|Q6653}}, and for {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} ({{jalink|1573|Q6678}} - In {{jalink|1593|Q6772}}), the {{jalink|sha territory}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1663|Q7577}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{jalink|Edo Shogunate|Q205662|Bakufu}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}10 ({{jalink|1697|Q7702}}), but {{jalink|Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|Mashinori}}5 years ({{jalink|1715|Q6884}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{jalink|Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 14 ({{jalink|1729|Q6952}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{jalink|Kato clan|Q10900894}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{jalink|Tarumitongu}} was located for the group of {{jalink|Saikū|Q150845|Prince Sainai}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{jalink|Ninwa|Q1346317}} 2nd year ({{jalink|886|Q24683}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{jalink|Ekima|Q65273644}} was permanently stationed there (``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448|Engishiki|Engishiki|Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{jalink|Tokaido|Q1196306}}.{{jalink|Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768| Mizuguchi}} has also had a {{jalink|shuku station}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{jalink|castle town|Q909458}} and also as a {{jalink|post town}} of {{jalink|53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}9), it was listed as {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|Gosha}}, and in {{jalink|1894|Q7843}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{jalink|1924|Q2173}} ({{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, it belongs to {{jalink|Shrine Headquarters|Q908077}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{jalink|Shake|Q11588367}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{jalink|Uchitaka clan}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{jalink|Miyaza|Q11454763}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{jalink|Yasu River|Q8049935}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{jalink|April 20th|Q2524}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{jalink|monkey}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{jalink|Gregorian calendar|Q12138|solar calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{jalink|1896|Q7846}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{jalink|Hikiyama}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{jalink|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}. Also, from around the {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} period (late 17th century), {{jalink|rain praying|Q477738|rain praying festival}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{jalink|1897|Q7847}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}30). Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Takemikazuchijin}}, {{jalink|Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{jalink|Ebisu Shrine}} - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Kotoshiro Nushikami}} * {{jalink|Hie Shrine|Q4189425}} - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105}}・{{jalink|Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{jalink|Kohka|Q1325317}} ({{jalink|1844|Q7633}}). {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{jalink|shadow|Q8364380}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. {{jalink|1911|Q2076}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{jalink|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552}}, {{jalink|1950|Q185 97}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{jalink|Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502}}, it became {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{jalink|Yasunaga|Q980266}} ({{jalink|1342|Q6069}}) of the {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{jalink|1882|Q7812}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{jalink|Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149}}" also includes "{{jalink|Daidoruijuhou|Q114 33347}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}} sent {{jalink|Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{jalink|Ohmi Railway|Q3276327}}{{jalink|Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{jalink|Kogakkan University}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{jalink|Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535}} supervised by {{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{jalink|Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}, 1945) *{{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites" Volume 5 Yamashiro and Omi [Reprinted], {{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02505-5 (first edition was published in 1986 ISBN) 4-560-02215-1) *"Place Names of Shiga Prefecture" (Japan Historical Place Names), Heibonsha, 1991 ISBN 4-582-49025-5 === External link === {{Commonscat|Minakuchi-jinja (Koka)|Mizuguchi Shrine}} * {{official|https://minakuchi-jinja.com/}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=547 Mizuguchi Shrine] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Minakuchishinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:甲賀市の歴史]] [[Category:甲賀市の建築物]] [[Category:甲賀市の重要文化財]] [[Category:水口藩]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]
