link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizuguchi Shrine |Image = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Koka City|Q847584|WD=Q847584|1=Kōka, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706|WD=Q3314706|1=Minakuchi, Shiga}}Miyanomae 3-14 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees, minutes, seconds|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizuguchi Shrine (Koka City)}} |Enshrined deity = Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} morning |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=April 20th|Q2524|WD=Q2524|1=April 20}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Mizuguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minakuchi Shrine'' (Minakuchi Shrine) is located in front of the shrine in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Koka City|Q847584|WD=Q847584|1=Kōka, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706|WD=Q3314706|1=Minakuchi, Shiga}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}. The old company rank is {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Prefectural company|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The annual festival is the {{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fujikawa|1=UNKNOWN}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{ill|lt=Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375|WD=Q1656375|1=Ichijō Kaneyoshi}} who was evacuated to {{ill|lt=Nara|Q11444975|WD=Q11444975|1=Nara}} due to the {{ill|lt=Onin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{ill|lt=1473|Q6594|WD=Q6594|1=1473}}) of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{ill|lt=Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318|WD=Q3469318|1=Saitō Myōchin}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{ill|lt=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966|WD=Q85883966|1=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{ill|lt=Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|WD=Q11436785|lt=Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto|1=Oomizuguchi no Sukune}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otomi Takamikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Sojinson|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Hozumi clan|Q11597079|WD=Q11597079|lt=Hozumiomi|1=Hozumi clan}}, who is a member of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|lt=Surnames and Names and Records|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}' {{ill|lt=Sakyo|Q11479660|WD=Q11479660|1=Sakyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamibetsu|Q11588768|WD=Q11588768|1=Shinbetsu}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishi Shinmyoki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'s {{ill|lt=Koga District|Q4227244|WD=Q4227244|1=Kōka District, Shiga}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|WD=Q72183108|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine (Q72183108)}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kuji Honki|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{ill|lt=Great Japanese History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Great Japanese History Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}", "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho|1=UNKNOWN}}", {{ill|lt=Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} ``{{ill|lt=Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}'', {{ill|lt=Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}}, ``{{ill|lt=Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatogahira Tumulus|1=UNKNOWN}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian deity|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, a famous temple of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kansei|1=UNKNOWN}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Shrine|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, which is the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Enryakuji|Q917195|WD=Q917195|1=Enryaku-ji}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Inadahime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{ill|lt=Omi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi|1=Ōmi Province}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{ill|lt=Yatabe clan|UNKNOWN|lt=Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokahikumogu|1=UNKNOWN}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=envoys|1=UNKNOWN}} to {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} Directly based on "{{ill|lt=Wahime no Miki|Q131331326|WD=Q131331326|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Motoise#List|Q11387775|WD=Q11387775|lt=Motoise|1=Moto-Ise Shrines}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{ill|lt=Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{ill|lt=Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}} - Most of it was lost in {{ill|lt=1569|Q6653|WD=Q6653|1=1569}}, and for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}} - In {{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|WD=Q6772|1=1593}}), the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sha territory|1=UNKNOWN}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1663|Q7577|WD=Q7577|1=1663}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}}10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}), but {{ill|lt=Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Mashinori|1=Shōtoku (era)}}5 years ({{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|WD=Q6884|1=1715}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{ill|lt=Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105|WD=Q11399105|1=Yoshinori Kato}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{ill|lt=Kato clan|Q10900894|WD=Q10900894|1=Katō clan}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tarumitongu|1=UNKNOWN}} was located for the group of {{ill|lt=Saikū|Q150845|WD=Q150845|lt=Prince Sainai|1=Saikū}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{ill|lt=Ninwa|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683|1=886}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{ill|lt=Ekima|Q65273644|WD=Q65273644|1=ekiba}} was permanently stationed there (``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}.{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768|WD=Q3069768|lt=Mizuguchi|1=Minakuchi-juku}} has also had a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuku station|1=UNKNOWN}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{ill|lt=castle town|Q909458|WD=Q909458|1=Jōkamachi}} and also as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=post town|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}9), it was listed as {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gosha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in {{ill|lt=1894|Q7843|WD=Q7843|1=1894}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|1=World War II}}, it belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uchitaka clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{ill|lt=Miyaza|Q11454763|WD=Q11454763|1=Miyaza}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{ill|lt=Yasu River|Q8049935|WD=Q8049935|1=Yasu River}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{ill|lt=April 20th|Q2524|WD=Q2524|1=April 20}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=monkey|1=UNKNOWN}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q12138|WD=Q12138|lt=solar calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikiyama|1=UNKNOWN}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}. Also, from around the {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} period (late 17th century), {{ill|lt=rain praying|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=rain praying festival|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}30). Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchijin|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebisu Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshiro Nushikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Hie Shrine|Q4189425|WD=Q4189425|1=Hie Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}・{{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kohka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} ({{ill|lt=1844|Q7633|WD=Q7633|1=1844}}). {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{ill|lt=shadow|Q8364380|WD=Q8364380|1=Yi}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1950|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}}, it became {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} ({{ill|lt=1342|Q6069|WD=Q6069|1=1342}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{ill|lt=Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149|WD=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}}" also includes "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Daidoruijuhou|1=UNKNOWN}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{ill|lt=Imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} sent {{ill|lt=Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646|WD=Q22128646|1=Masayoshi Uematsu}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Ohmi Railway|Q3276327|WD=Q3276327|1=Ohmi Railway}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422|WD=Q3184422|1=Minakuchi Jōnan Station}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University|1=UNKNOWN}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{ill|lt=Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} supervised by {{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{ill|lt=Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1945) *{{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites" Volume 5 Yamashiro and Omi [Reprinted], {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02505-5 (first edition was published in 1986 ISBN) 4-560-02215-1) *"Place Names of Shiga Prefecture" (Japan Historical Place Names), Heibonsha, 1991 ISBN 4-582-49025-5 === External link === {{Commonscat|Minakuchi-jinja (Koka)|Mizuguchi Shrine}} * {{official|https://minakuchi-jinja.com/}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=547 Mizuguchi Shrine] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:甲賀市の歴史]] [[Category:甲賀市の建築物]] [[Category:甲賀市の重要文化財]] [[Category:水口藩]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

The old company rank is {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Prefectural company|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The annual festival is the {{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fujikawa|1=UNKNOWN}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{ill|lt=Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375|WD=Q1656375|1=Ichijō Kaneyoshi}} who was evacuated to {{ill|lt=Nara|Q11444975|WD=Q11444975|1=Nara}} due to the {{ill|lt=Onin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{ill|lt=1473|Q6594|WD=Q6594|1=1473}}) of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{ill|lt=Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318|WD=Q3469318|1=Saitō Myōchin}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{ill|lt=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966|WD=Q85883966|1=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{ill|lt=Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|WD=Q11436785|lt=Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto|1=Oomizuguchi no Sukune}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otomi Takamikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Sojinson|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Hozumi clan|Q11597079|WD=Q11597079|lt=Hozumiomi|1=Hozumi clan}}, who is a member of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|lt=Surnames and Names and Records|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}' {{ill|lt=Sakyo|Q11479660|WD=Q11479660|1=Sakyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamibetsu|Q11588768|WD=Q11588768|1=Shinbetsu}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishi Shinmyoki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'s {{ill|lt=Koga District|Q4227244|WD=Q4227244|1=Kōka District, Shiga}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|WD=Q72183108|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine (Q72183108)}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kuji Honki|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{ill|lt=Great Japanese History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Great Japanese History Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}", "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho|1=UNKNOWN}}", {{ill|lt=Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} ``{{ill|lt=Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}'', {{ill|lt=Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}}, ``{{ill|lt=Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatogahira Tumulus|1=UNKNOWN}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian deity|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, a famous temple of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kansei|1=UNKNOWN}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Shrine|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, which is the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Enryakuji|Q917195|WD=Q917195|1=Enryaku-ji}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Inadahime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{ill|lt=Omi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi|1=Ōmi Province}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{ill|lt=Yatabe clan|UNKNOWN|lt=Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokahikumogu|1=UNKNOWN}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=envoys|1=UNKNOWN}} to {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} Directly based on "{{ill|lt=Wahime no Miki|Q131331326|WD=Q131331326|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Motoise#List|Q11387775|WD=Q11387775|lt=Motoise|1=Moto-Ise Shrines}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{ill|lt=Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{ill|lt=Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}} - Most of it was lost in {{ill|lt=1569|Q6653|WD=Q6653|1=1569}}, and for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}} - In {{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|WD=Q6772|1=1593}}), the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sha territory|1=UNKNOWN}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1663|Q7577|WD=Q7577|1=1663}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}}10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}), but {{ill|lt=Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Mashinori|1=Shōtoku (era)}}5 years ({{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|WD=Q6884|1=1715}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{ill|lt=Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105|WD=Q11399105|1=Yoshinori Kato}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{ill|lt=Kato clan|Q10900894|WD=Q10900894|1=Katō clan}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tarumitongu|1=UNKNOWN}} was located for the group of {{ill|lt=Saikū|Q150845|WD=Q150845|lt=Prince Sainai|1=Saikū}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{ill|lt=Ninwa|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683|1=886}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{ill|lt=Ekima|Q65273644|WD=Q65273644|1=ekiba}} was permanently stationed there (``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}.{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768|WD=Q3069768|lt=Mizuguchi|1=Minakuchi-juku}} has also had a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuku station|1=UNKNOWN}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{ill|lt=castle town|Q909458|WD=Q909458|1=Jōkamachi}} and also as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=post town|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}9), it was listed as {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gosha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in {{ill|lt=1894|Q7843|WD=Q7843|1=1894}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|1=World War II}}, it belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uchitaka clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{ill|lt=Miyaza|Q11454763|WD=Q11454763|1=Miyaza}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{ill|lt=Yasu River|Q8049935|WD=Q8049935|1=Yasu River}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{ill|lt=April 20th|Q2524|WD=Q2524|1=April 20}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=monkey|1=UNKNOWN}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q12138|WD=Q12138|lt=solar calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikiyama|1=UNKNOWN}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}. Also, from around the {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} period (late 17th century), {{ill|lt=rain praying|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=rain praying festival|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}30). Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchijin|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebisu Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshiro Nushikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Hie Shrine|Q4189425|WD=Q4189425|1=Hie Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}・{{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kohka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} ({{ill|lt=1844|Q7633|WD=Q7633|1=1844}}). {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{ill|lt=shadow|Q8364380|WD=Q8364380|1=Yi}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1950|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}}, it became {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} ({{ill|lt=1342|Q6069|WD=Q6069|1=1342}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{ill|lt=Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149|WD=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}}" also includes "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Daidoruijuhou|1=UNKNOWN}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{ill|lt=Imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} sent {{ill|lt=Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646|WD=Q22128646|1=Masayoshi Uematsu}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Ohmi Railway|Q3276327|WD=Q3276327|1=Ohmi Railway}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422|WD=Q3184422|1=Minakuchi Jōnan Station}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University|1=UNKNOWN}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{ill|lt=Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} supervised by {{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{ill|lt=Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1945) *{{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites" Volume 5 Yamashiro and Omi [Reprinted], {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 2000 ISBN 978-4-560-02505-5 (first edition was published in 1986 ISBN) 4-560-02215-1) *"Place Names of Shiga Prefecture" (Japan Historical Place Names), Heibonsha, 1991 ISBN 4-582-49025-5 === External link === {{Commonscat|Minakuchi-jinja (Koka)|Mizuguchi Shrine}} * {{official|https://minakuchi-jinja.com/}} * [http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=547 Mizuguchi Shrine] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Minakuchi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:近江国の式内社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧県社]] [[Category:甲賀市の歴史]] [[Category:甲賀市の建築物]] [[Category:甲賀市の重要文化財]] [[Category:水口藩]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

link|Q11548384}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizuguchi Shrine |Image = [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
House of worship |Location= {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Koka City|Q847584|WD=Q847584|1=Kōka, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706|WD=Q3314706|1=Minakuchi, Shiga}}Miyanomae 3-14 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees, minutes, seconds|34|58|03|N|136|10|03|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mizuguchi Shrine (Koka City)}} |Enshrined deity = Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} morning |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=April 20th|Q2524|WD=Q2524|1=April 20}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shiga|label=Mizuguchi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minakuchi Shrine'' (Minakuchi Shrine) is located in front of the shrine in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Koka City|Q847584|WD=Q847584|1=Kōka, Shiga}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Town|Q3314706|WD=Q3314706|1=Minakuchi, Shiga}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}. The old company rank is {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Prefectural company|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The annual festival is the {{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}. === Company name === In the Middle Ages, it was called ``Sanno Shingu'' (``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fujikawa|1=UNKNOWN}}'' May 24th article says, ``If you pass Minakuchi (omitted), you will reach Shingu's Baba (omitted), and Shingu will be at Sanno.'' Kawaki'' is written by {{ill|lt=Kaneyoshi Ichijo|Q1656375|WD=Q1656375|1=Ichijō Kaneyoshi}} who was evacuated to {{ill|lt=Nara|Q11444975|WD=Q11444975|1=Nara}} due to the {{ill|lt=Onin War|Q385676|WD=Q385676|1=Ōnin War}}. (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} in the year 5 ({{ill|lt=1473|Q6594|WD=Q6594|1=1473}}) of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} k|Guodai|Q568360}}{{ill|lt=Myotsubaki Saito|Q3469318|WD=Q3469318|1=Saitō Myōchin}} This is a travelogue of the one month he traveled to the country until his return to Nara. ``By the way,'' the following should be deleted at the dawn of the entry in ``Fujikawa (River) Chronicles'' -->) In the early modern period, it was called ``Omiya Daimyo Shrine'' (``{{ill|lt=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku|Q85883966|WD=Q85883966|1=Omi Koshiji Shiryaku}}''), and even today it is called ``Omiya-san'' locally. === Enshrined God === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (2).jpg|thumb|main hall]] * Main enshrined deity - {{ill|lt=Ominakuchi Sukune|Q11436785|WD=Q11436785|lt=Ōminakuchi Sukune no Mikoto|1=Oomizuguchi no Sukune}} (Ominakuchi no Sukune no Mikoto) * Aidonojin - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otomi Takamikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Sojinson|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto is said to be the founder of the {{ill|lt=Hozumi clan|Q11597079|WD=Q11597079|lt=Hozumiomi|1=Hozumi clan}}, who is a member of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}}. 935}}' Emperor Suinin's March 25 AD Ichiunjo, '{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surnames and Names Record|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|lt=Surnames and Names and Records|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}' {{ill|lt=Sakyo|Q11479660|WD=Q11479660|1=Sakyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamibetsu|Q11588768|WD=Q11588768|1=Shinbetsu}} Hozumi Omijo, etc. , {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishi Shinmyoki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'s {{ill|lt=Koga District|Q4227244|WD=Q4227244|1=Kōka District, Shiga}} states that ``Kawakare Shrine 2 {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine, currently located at 1607-1, Mizuga, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City (Koka City)|Q72183108|WD=Q72183108|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine (Q72183108)}} has been identified as ``'''Kawakaren no Kami'' in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kuji Honki|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is believed that this shrine is related to ``Ahamikawa Karehime'', the grandmother of Omizuguchi Sukune no Mikoto, who is included in the Mononobe clan genealogy, and it is said that the Mononobe clan, which lived in this area, enshrined their ancestors. ref> "{{ill|lt=Great Japanese History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Great Japanese History Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}", "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Shinmei Gohisho|1=UNKNOWN}}", {{ill|lt=Nobutomo Ban|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} ``{{ill|lt=Shinmeichō koshododai|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}'', {{ill|lt=Suzuka Rentane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}}, ``{{ill|lt=Shinja Kaoroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'', etc. . The entire enshrinement area is located in the center of the former Koga District, and there are several late-stage burial mounds such as the largest {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatogahira Tumulus|1=UNKNOWN}} in the surrounding area. The fact that the area is scattered here and there indicates that the area has been open since ancient times, and judging from the names of the shrines and deities it enshrines, it is assumed that the area also played the role of the god of agriculture, who controlled irrigation water, from an early period."Japanese Gods", 1986 (Ryojun Mitsuda "Mizuguchi Shrine Kawada Shrine"). .Furthermore, the three pillars that are currently considered to be the main hall were once considered the main deities enshrined in the first year of the {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} era (early 18th century), but they appear to be in the ``Ishio Family Diary,'' a diary kept by successive generations of the Ishio family of the shrine family. , because in the medieval times this shrine was said to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian deity|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, a famous temple of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}} near which this shrine is located. It is thought to be "Toyugyo Kasho," but it is unconfirmed --> and the above-mentioned "Shinmyocho Kousho Doshiro." , it is thought to be the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kansei|1=UNKNOWN}} of Daiki no Mikoto of the Nishihongu Shrine of {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|Q656451|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Shrine|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, which is the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Enryakuji|Q917195|WD=Q917195|1=Enryaku-ji}}, and also added two pillars that are said to be its parent deities. name="Investigation Report"> "Shikinaisha Investigation Report", 1981 (Shigeki Uno "Mizuguchi Shrine"). . === History ===According to the shrine legend, in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Inadahime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}} was {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q4556} 02}} entered {{ill|lt=Omi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi|1=Ōmi Province}} to worship the divine mirror, and the clan chief, {{ill|lt=Yatabe clan|UNKNOWN|lt=Yatabe Sukune}} Kuratamaro (Yatabe no Sukune) ) was asked about the place where the divine mirror should be enshrined, and the answer was that the best place was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokahikumogu|1=UNKNOWN}} (Koga no Hikumonomiya). 049935}}) upstream to the current shrine site and worshiped the divine mirror for four years, as well as enshrining Otomi no Mikoto as the god of protection of the divine mirror. It is said that ``Wahime no Mikoto'' in the enshrinement tradition of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, where Amaterasu Omikami traveled around the country worshiping the divine mirror of Amaterasu Omikami, was changed to ``Inada Hime no Mikoto.'' Since ancient times, they have had a close relationship with each other, with the ``Ise Oji Road'' used by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=envoys|1=UNKNOWN}} to {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} passing through, and as a result, in later generations, the shrine's {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} Directly based on "{{ill|lt=Wahime no Miki|Q131331326|WD=Q131331326|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}" brought about by the advocacy activities of {jalink|Oshi|Q11488972}}, In the 4th year of Emperor Suinin's life in Wahime no Mikojo, it is written that ``Transferred to Kōka Nichiungu in four years, and at Uji, Ami Province was established at Shinchiguchi Ota'', which tells of a four-year service in Koka District, and the traditional site of Kōka Nichiungu also exists in the Mizuguchi Town area. For more information, please refer to "Omi Province Kokahiungu Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Motoise#List|Q11387775|WD=Q11387775|lt=Motoise|1=Moto-Ise Shrines}}. It is thought that it may have been made by . Therefore, as mentioned above, it is believed that the shrine was originally created by the local Mononobe clan, who enshrined their ancestors (the Yatabe clan, which appears in the shrine's legend, is also a member of the same clan as the Mononobe clan. '{{ill|lt=Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} (Sakyojinbetsu-jo, Yamato no Kunijinbetsu, etc.), 'Kyujihonki' Tenson Honki.)."{{ill|lt=Japan's Three Dynasty Chronicles|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On January 27, 1985 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), it appears that he was promoted from Jugoi Lower to Jugoi Upper, and was listed as a Kokuheisho shrine in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engi system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Although other historical materials are lacking, as mentioned above, in the medieval period it was called "Sanno Shingu" as the guardian deity of {{ill|lt=Ookaji|Q60988301|WD=Q60988301|1=Ookaji}}, and according to the shrine's legend, it had a vast precinct area of 30 towns in the east and west, and 25 towns in the north and south, and for a period of {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}} - Most of it was lost in {{ill|lt=1569|Q6653|WD=Q6653|1=1569}}, and for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}} - In {{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|WD=Q6772|1=1593}}), the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sha territory|1=UNKNOWN}}, which had 130 koku, was also confiscated, and after that, the shrine only had a narrow precinct area of 4 to 5 ken squared. In {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1663|Q7577|WD=Q7577|1=1663}}), Niemon Kobori, the magistrate of {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}, became a local citizen. He ordered the shrine to be rebuilt, and there is a record that the shrine was repaired in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}}10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}), but {{ill|lt=Mashinori (Japan)|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Mashinori|1=Shōtoku (era)}}5 years ({{ill|lt=1715|Q6884|WD=Q6884|1=1715}}) {{jalink|Mizuguchi clan|Q1086350 4|When {{ill|lt=Kato Yoshinori|Q11399105|WD=Q11399105|1=Yoshinori Kato}}, who was returned to Japan as the lord of Mizuguchi, entered Japan for the first time, he lamented the devastation of this shrine and asked the townspeople to visit the shrine. It is said that the shrine was built in September of {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}}), and since then it has been under the protection of {{ill|lt=Kato clan|Q10900894|WD=Q10900894|1=Katō clan}} throughout the feudal period. name="Investigation Report"/>. In ancient times, Koga District was accessed by the above-mentioned Ise Oji Road, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tarumitongu|1=UNKNOWN}} was located for the group of {{ill|lt=Saikū|Q150845|WD=Q150845|lt=Prince Sainai|1=Saikū}}. September 30th, 2017 ({{ill|lt=Ninwa|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683|1=886}}) of the Sandai Jitsuroku. In addition, Koga Station was established and {{ill|lt=Ekima|Q65273644|WD=Q65273644|1=ekiba}} was permanently stationed there (``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}''), making it an important location on the {{ill|lt=Tokaido|Q1196306|WD=Q1196306|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}.{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi-juku|Q3069768|WD=Q3069768|lt=Mizuguchi|1=Minakuchi-juku}} has also had a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuku station|1=UNKNOWN}} since the Middle Ages, but since the return of the Kato clan, it has prospered as its {{ill|lt=castle town|Q909458|WD=Q909458|1=Jōkamachi}} and also as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=post town|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=53 stations of the Tokaido|Q3134262|WD=Q3134262|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}}, and along with that, our shrine has also flourished greatly.In October {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}9), it was listed as {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Gosha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in {{ill|lt=1894|Q7843|WD=Q7843|1=1894}} (Meiji 27) The shrine was expanded, and the construction of the shrine building was completed in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} (Meiji 30), and in June of {{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}13) it was promoted to a prefectural shrine. After {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|1=World War II}}, it belongs to {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === Shinto priest === Throughout the early modern period, the Sekio family occupied the position of chief priest as {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}}. The Ishio clan, whose real surname is the Yatabe clan, is said to have been a member of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uchitaka clan|1=UNKNOWN}}, a local magnate from Kitauchi Takashi. === Rituals === [[File:Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival.jpg|thumb|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival]] {{main|Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival}} The center of the Minakuchi district, which is present-day Honmachi, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, Matsue, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, and Kabuka, Minakuchi-cho, Koka City, is considered a parishioner area, but in early modern times, this area was divided into two districts, Minakuchi in the east and Minobe in the west, and {{ill|lt=Miyaza|Q11454763|WD=Q11454763|1=Miyaza}} was organized in each district. In the Mizuguchi area, 30 elderly people were selected to serve at the Omiya (head office), and one person, known as Kodo, presided over Shinto rituals. In the Minobe area, Setsusha Gobu Shrine (currently Takeo Shrine) was designated as the guardian deity of Minobe, and 10 elderly people and one shrine, like Mizuguchi, were selected to preside over the rituals. Kitanai Takashi, which borders the south across the {{ill|lt=Yasu River|Q8049935|WD=Q8049935|1=Yasu River}}, was also treated as a parishioner area and organized a shrine, but this is thought to be because Kitauchi Takashi formed one village with the Minakuchi-Minobu hamlet back in the Middle Ages. These Miyaza continued until the beginning of the Meiji era, but were abolished in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6).In the 6th year of the Meiji era of the ``Sekio Family Diary'' mentioned above, it is written that ``the Omiya Village Four Troupes, the Takeo Troupe, and the Naiki Troupe will be abolished.'' .The main ritual of this shrine is the ``Mizuguchi Festival'' held on {{ill|lt=April 20th|Q2524|WD=Q2524|1=April 20}}. The festival date was anciently known as Kaminin Day in April, and it is said that this festival was set in honor of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=monkey|1=UNKNOWN}}, a divine messenger of Hiyoshi Shrine, from the time when it was called ``Sanno Shingu.'' name="Investigation report"/>, after the Meiji era, it was changed to {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q12138|WD=Q12138|lt=solar calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} and the rituals were also changed, and the current date was set in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29). On that day, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikiyama|1=UNKNOWN}} are dedicated from within the town, so it is also called '''{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival|Q11548378|WD=Q11548378|1=Minakuchi Hikiyama Festival}}''' and is designated as a prefecture-designated {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}. Also, from around the {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} period (late 17th century), {{ill|lt=rain praying|Q477738|WD=Q477738|lt=rain praying festival|1=Rainmaking (ritual)}} began to be held in response to drought, and on those occasions, the lord of the feudal lord would worship or visit on his behalf, according to the Shiga Prefecture Shrine Agency website. . === Precincts === [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (6).jpg|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Minakuchi-jinja 2021-07 ac (4).jpg|thumb|Precinct]] * Main shrine - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}30). Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. * Oryosha * Takeo Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchijin|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsujinjin|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. As mentioned above, in early modern times it was considered the guardian deity of Minobe, and the western Miyaza was in charge of Shinto rituals. * Tamatsumae Shrine - Enshrined deity: Tamatsumai Reison * Mizuguchi {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebisu Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshiro Nushikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Hie Shrine|Q4189425|WD=Q4189425|1=Hie Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}・{{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kohka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} ({{ill|lt=1844|Q7633|WD=Q7633|1=1844}}). {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}. * Ema-dono - There are few shrines in Omi that have an ema-den within the precincts, making this a valuable example. * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} * Shrine office * Taikobashi === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === * Wooden Seated Statue of the Goddess (Joshinzazo) - Colored statue with a height of 22.2cm Statue height is based on Mainichi Shimbun's ``Encyclopedia of National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties,'' Ken Kuno, ed., ``Illustrated Encyclopedia of Buddhist Statue Pilgrimages'' (Newly Revised Edition, Yamakawa Publishing, 1994). . Her hair is down, her hands are tucked into her sleeves, and she is believed to have originally held a {{ill|lt=shadow|Q8364380|WD=Q8364380|1=Yi}} (sashiha) --> with her hands tied at the tip of her chest. The statue has a simple style, with no carvings on the folds of his clothes, and no features on the front of his knees. Estimated to have been created in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}} Designated as a national treasure (former national treasure) on August 9, 1911 (Meiji 44) based on {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1950|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}25) Due to the enforcement of {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}}, it became {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. === Shiga Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Preservation Promotion Association) === Shiga Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Mizuguchi Hikiyama Festival (Mizuguchi Festival Hikiyama Preservation Society) === Koka City designated tangible cultural property === * Stone lantern - There is an inscription of the first year of {{ill|lt=Yasunaga|Q980266|WD=Q980266|1=Kōei}} ({{ill|lt=1342|Q6069|WD=Q6069|1=1342}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, which reveals the year of manufacture. === Others === * A medicine called "Mizuguchi medicine" was once passed down as a family tradition in the Sekio family of the shrine family, and in {{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}} (Meiji 15), it was given the name "Antaisan" by the Ministry of Home Affairs. (Japan)|Q1142265|Ministry of Home Affairs}} Approved, "{{ill|lt=Great Japan Place Name Dictionary|Q17213149|WD=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}}" also includes "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Daidoruijuhou|1=UNKNOWN}}, and wrote, ``Mizuguchi medicine, Omi Province Mizuguchi Shrine celebration Tono no Kaden, originator Omi Takajin Rikiya, heat and cold, daily and occasional thirst, and intestinal motility effect, etc.'', but it has not been passed down at present. * In the diary of the Shake Sekio family, we can see that in September {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} (first year of the Meiji era), on the occasion of the Emperor Toyuki, {{ill|lt=Imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} sent {{ill|lt=Masayoshi Uematsu|Q22128646|WD=Q22128646|1=Masayoshi Uematsu}} to this shrine. === Transportation access === * 10 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Ohmi Railway|Q3276327|WD=Q3276327|1=Ohmi Railway}}{{ill|lt=Mizuguchi Jonan Station|Q3184422|WD=Q3184422|1=Minakuchi Jōnan Station}} === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *Shikinaisha Research Report, Volume 12, edited by Shikinaisha Research Association, Omi Province, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kogakkan University|1=UNKNOWN}} Publishing Department, 1982. *{{ill|lt=Naoichi Miyaji|Q11453535|WD=Q11453535|1=Naokazu Miyaji}} supervised by {{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, reduced edition", {{ill|lt=Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1968 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1945) *{{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} "Japanese Gods -
